hypotheses.claim: The Voynich glyphs encode a homophonic substitution cipher on medieval Latin, where the 25 glyphs map to a Latin alphabet expanded with homophones for high-frequency letters (e, a, i, t, s), reducing glyph entropy below the expected Latin ~4.0 bits to the observed 3.8627 bits.
evidence_for: Glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is slightly below Latin ~4.0 bits, consistent with homophonic expansion flattening the frequency distribution
Word-initial constraint set {o,c,q,s,d} and word-final set {y,n,l,r,o} could reflect Latin morphological endings masked by homophones
Multiple high-confidence beliefs already support a homophonic cipher framework
Currier A/B cipher-table divergence is consistent with two homophonic tables for the same plaintext
evidence_against: Hapax ratio 70.1% is far higher than expected for homophonic Latin, which should reduce not increase type diversity
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is lower than typical natural-language Latin (~1.0), suggesting either non-Latin or added noise
Word entropy 10.4508 bits exceeds typical enciphered Latin word distributions
hypotheses.test: Compute the bigram entropy and conditional entropy H(G2|G1) for all adjacent glyph pairs; compare to theoretical homophonic Latin bigram structure. Under homophonic substitution on Latin, H(G2|G1) should be lower than the unconditioned glyph entropy (3.8627) and should match shuffled Latin bigram profiles within 0.05 bits.
hypotheses.test_metric: Conditional bigram entropy H(G2|G1) across full corpus and separately for Currier A and B sub-corpora
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirm if H(G2|G1) < 3.6 bits and the ratio H(G2|G1)/H(G1) falls in [0.88, 0.95], matching Latin homophonic profiles; deny if H(G2|G1) >= 3.8 bits (no redundancy exploited by bigrams)
tests_remaining: Compute the bigram entropy and conditional entropy H(G2|G1) for all adjacent glyph pairs; compare to theoretical homophonic Latin bigram structure. Under homophonic substitution on Latin, H(G2|G1) should be lower than the unconditioned glyph entropy (3.8627) and should match shuffled Latin bigram profiles within 0.05 bits.
hypotheses.reasoning: A homophonic cipher on Latin would produce the observed entropy suppression and glyph-positional patterns while accommodating the Currier A/B split as two distinct homophonic tables. Measuring conditional bigram entropy directly tests whether sequential glyph pairs carry the correlation structure expected from an underlying inflected language.

hypotheses.claim: The anomalously high hapax ratio (70.1%) is substantially produced by a systematic word-final suffix drawn from the set {y, n, l, r, o} that is a cipher artifact (e.g., a null, padding, or word-delimiter glyph), such that stripping that final glyph from all words reduces the unique-word count by at least 30%.
evidence_for: Word-final glyphs {y,n,l,r,o} account for the dominant positional constraint, suggesting a structural rather than grammatical role
High hapax ratio is inconsistent with natural-language Zipf scaling at exponent 0.8946, pointing to an artificial source of surface type diversity
Existing belief at 0.54 already partially supports suffix-stripping reducing hapax count
If final glyphs are padding or nulls, stripping them would collapse many hapax forms to shared stems
evidence_against: Stripping final glyphs from Latin or Italian words would also be linguistically valid (case/conjugation endings), so reduction might reflect grammar not artifact
Word-final {y,n,l,r,o} could encode real Latin endings (-us, -um, -is, -am, -re)
Recipes section has the largest word count and second-highest entropy, suggesting some sections have genuine suffix variation
hypotheses.test: For each unique word form ending in any glyph from {y,n,l,r,o}, create a stemmed vocabulary by removing the final glyph; compute new vocabulary size, new hapax count, and new hapax ratio. Run the same procedure on the Currier A and B sub-corpora separately.
hypotheses.test_metric: Reduction in hapax ratio after final-glyph stripping: (original_hapax_count - stemmed_hapax_count) / original_hapax_count
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirm artifact hypothesis if stripping reduces hapax ratio by >= 30 percentage points (from 70.1% to <= 40%); deny if reduction < 10 percentage points, suggesting final glyphs encode real morphological information
tests_remaining: For each unique word form ending in any glyph from {y,n,l,r,o}, create a stemmed vocabulary by removing the final glyph; compute new vocabulary size, new hapax count, and new hapax ratio. Run the same procedure on the Currier A and B sub-corpora separately.
hypotheses.reasoning: If the hapax explosion is artificial and caused by a trailing null or padding glyph, a single suffix-stripping pass should dramatically collapse the vocabulary. This test is fully computable from the word list and would immediately distinguish cipher artifact from natural morphological diversity.

hypotheses.claim: The text-only section (7 folios, entropy 3.9016 bits â€” highest of all sections) represents a functionally distinct register encoded with minimal or no transposition, i.e., a near-plaintext or lightly enciphered layer, while lower-entropy sections (zodiac 3.7149, astronomical 3.7471) use additional transposition steps that suppress entropy.
evidence_for: Text-only entropy 3.9016 bits is closest to natural Latin/Italian (~3.95-4.0), suggesting least transformation
Zodiac entropy 3.7149 bits is lowest, consistent with label-dominated, highly repetitive encoding adding a transposition or nulling step
Section entropy ordering (text-only > recipes > herbal > cosmological > biological > pharmaceutical > astronomical > zodiac) correlates with expected register complexity
High-confidence existing belief (0.972) already supports text-only as closest to natural language
evidence_against: Lower entropy in zodiac could reflect label brevity and small vocabulary rather than additional cipher steps
Entropy differences across sections (~0.19 bits range) are modest and could arise from topic-specific vocabulary differences without any cipher variation
The cosmological section has more folios than astronomical yet similar entropy, complicating a simple transposition-step model
hypotheses.test: Compute the word-type/token ratio (TTR) and word-length distribution for each section independently; run a Mann-Whitney U test comparing text-only TTR and mean word length against all other sections combined. Additionally compute section-level Zipf exponents and compare to text-only.
hypotheses.test_metric: Per-section TTR, mean word length, Zipf exponent, and entropy; Mann-Whitney U p-value for text-only vs. all other sections on each metric
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirm structural distinctness if text-only Zipf exponent is >= 0.95 and all other sections are <= 0.90, and Mann-Whitney p < 0.05 on TTR; deny if text-only metrics fall within one standard deviation of the cross-section mean
tests_remaining: Compute the word-type/token ratio (TTR) and word-length distribution for each section independently; run a Mann-Whitney U test comparing text-only TTR and mean word length against all other sections combined. Additionally compute section-level Zipf exponents and compare to text-only.
hypotheses.reasoning: If the text-only section is a distinct, less-enciphered register, it should show not just higher entropy but also different word-length distributions and a Zipf slope closer to natural language. Confirming this would justify using text-only folios as the primary decipherment target.

hypotheses.claim: Currier A and Currier B employ two structurally distinct cipher alphabets in which a core set of glyphs is shared but a subset of approximately 5-8 glyphs is exclusive or predominantly exclusive to each sub-corpus, producing measurable vocabulary intersection below 60% when controlling for word length.
evidence_for: Multiple high-confidence beliefs (0.943, 0.944, 0.971) support two structurally distinct cipher tables for A and B
Currier B has more than twice the word count of A (23,766 vs 11,022), making glyph-frequency profiles statistically robust enough to identify exclusive glyphs
If cipher tables differ, words of identical length across A and B should show lower-than-chance overlap in glyph composition
evidence_against: Two scribes encoding the same language with the same cipher but different handwriting habits could produce apparent glyph-frequency divergence without a structural cipher difference
Shared positional constraints {o,c,q,s,d} / {y,n,l,r,o} appear in both sub-corpora, constraining how distinct the tables can be
hypotheses.test: Extract the per-glyph frequency distribution for Currier A and Currier B separately; compute Jensen-Shannon divergence between the two distributions. Then for each word length L from 3 to 8, compute the Jaccard similarity of the word-type sets in A vs B at that length.
hypotheses.test_metric: Jensen-Shannon divergence between Currier A and B glyph distributions; per-length-bucket Jaccard similarity of word types
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirm distinct cipher tables if JSD > 0.05 bits AND mean Jaccard similarity across length buckets < 0.35; deny if JSD < 0.02 bits and mean Jaccard > 0.55 (consistent with same cipher, different scribal hands only)
tests_remaining: Extract the per-glyph frequency distribution for Currier A and Currier B separately; compute Jensen-Shannon divergence between the two distributions. Then for each word length L from 3 to 8, compute the Jaccard similarity of the word-type sets in A vs B at that length.
hypotheses.reasoning: Two truly distinct cipher alphabets would leave a detectable fingerprint in glyph-frequency divergence and in low vocabulary overlap at matched word lengths, since the same plaintext words would be enciphered differently. This test distinguishes cipher-table divergence from mere handwriting variation.

hypotheses.claim: The Zipf exponent of 0.8946 (below the natural-language baseline of ~1.0) is caused by systematic word-level homophones: multiple surface word forms encode the same plaintext word, inflating low-frequency tail counts. Collapsing homophones defined by shared word-initial bigram and word length would restore the Zipf exponent to >= 0.95.
evidence_for: Zipf exponent 0.8946 with R-squared 0.9084 indicates a moderate but suboptimal fit, consistent with artificial inflation of the low-frequency tail
Homophonic ciphers are known to flatten Zipf slopes by distributing high-frequency plaintext words across multiple surface forms
Word entropy 10.4508 bits is high relative to glyph entropy 3.8627, suggesting surface word diversity exceeds what grammar alone would produce
evidence_against: R-squared 0.9084 is still a reasonable Zipf fit; the deviation from 1.0 could reflect topic-restricted medieval vocabulary rather than cipher homophones
Collapsing by initial bigram and length is an arbitrary grouping that may not correspond to true plaintext equivalence classes
Hapax ratio 70.1% under a homophonic model should decrease toward the tail, not inflate it, unless the homophone set is very large
hypotheses.test: Group all unique word forms by (word-initial bigram, word length) as a proxy for plaintext equivalence classes; compute the Zipf exponent of the collapsed frequency distribution (treating each group as one token type, summing its tokens). Compare collapsed Zipf exponent and R-squared to the original.
hypotheses.test_metric: Zipf exponent and R-squared of the collapsed word-frequency distribution versus the original (0.8946, RÂ²=0.9084)
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirm homophone hypothesis if collapsed Zipf exponent >= 0.95 and R-squared improves to >= 0.94; deny if collapsed exponent changes by < 0.02 or R-squared decreases, indicating the grouping variable does not capture real equivalence classes
tests_remaining: Group all unique word forms by (word-initial bigram, word length) as a proxy for plaintext equivalence classes; compute the Zipf exponent of the collapsed frequency distribution (treating each group as one token type, summing its tokens). Compare collapsed Zipf exponent and R-squared to the original.
hypotheses.reasoning: If homophones are the mechanism suppressing the Zipf exponent, then grouping by a simple cipher-structure proxy (initial bigram + length) should partially undo the suppression and recover a more natural frequency distribution. A measurable improvement in Zipf fit would be strong evidence for word-level homophony.
